stem: What best describes generative AI compared to traditional AI?
choice_a: Generative AI only performs classification tasks on structured data.
choice_b: Generative AI can create new content such as text, images, and audio, while traditional AI typically analyzes or predicts based on existing data.
choice_c: Generative AI is a subset of robotics used for physical automation.
choice_d: Traditional AI requires more data than generative AI to function effectively.
explanation: Generative AI produces new content (text, images, code, audio, video) rather than simply classifying or predicting. Traditional AI/ML models typically analyze existing data to detect patterns or make predictions without creating new artifacts.

stem: Which technology breakthrough primarily enabled the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs)?
choice_a: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
choice_b: The Transformer architecture introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need'
choice_c: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) with LSTM cells
choice_d: Support Vector Machines (SVMs)
explanation: The Transformer architecture (2017) introduced the self-attention mechanism, enabling models to process long sequences in parallel and capture long-range dependencies. This breakthrough underpins virtually all modern LLMs including GPT, Gemini, and BERT.

stem: In the machine learning lifecycle, what is the primary purpose of the 'evaluation' phase?
choice_a: Collecting and labeling training data
choice_b: Deploying the model to production infrastructure
choice_c: Measuring model performance against held-out data to assess generalization
choice_d: Selecting the cloud provider for hosting
explanation: Evaluation assesses how well a trained model generalizes to unseen data by measuring metrics like accuracy, F1-score, or BLEU on a held-out test set. This ensures the model performs well beyond the training data before deployment.

stem: What is 'overfitting' in machine learning?
choice_a: When a model is too simple to capture patterns in the data
choice_b: When a model learns training data too well, including noise, and performs poorly on new data
choice_c: When a model is trained on too little data
choice_d: When a model takes too long to train
explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model memorizes the training data, including noise and irrelevant details, resulting in excellent training performance but poor generalization to new, unseen examples. Techniques like regularization, dropout, and cross-validation help prevent it.

stem: What is a 'foundation model' in the context of generative AI?
choice_a: A small, task-specific model trained on a narrow dataset
choice_b: A large model pre-trained on vast amounts of data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks
choice_c: The first version of any machine learning model before fine-tuning
choice_d: A model exclusively used for image recognition
explanation: Foundation models are large-scale models pre-trained on broad, diverse datasets. They serve as a base that can be adapted (via fine-tuning or prompting) for many downstream tasks. Examples include Gemini, GPT-4, and BERT.

stem: What is the role of 'embeddings' in generative AI systems?
choice_a: Embeddings store raw user data in encrypted format
choice_b: Embeddings are dense vector representations that capture semantic meaning, enabling similarity search and retrieval
choice_c: Embeddings are used to compress model weights for deployment
choice_d: Embeddings convert images to text for processing
explanation: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data (text, images, audio) that capture semantic meaning in a high-dimensional space. Similar items have vectors close together, enabling semantic search, clustering, and retrieval-augmented generation.

stem: Which type of model generates images by learning to reverse a noise-addition process?
choice_a: Transformer model
choice_b: Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)
choice_c: Diffusion model
choice_d: Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
explanation: Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse a process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to training images. During inference, they start from pure noise and iteratively denoise to produce high-quality outputs. Imagen and Stable Diffusion use this approach.

stem: What does 'multimodal' mean in the context of AI models?
choice_a: The model can run on multiple cloud platforms simultaneously
choice_b: The model can process and generate multiple types of data such as text, images, audio, and video
choice_c: The model uses multiple training algorithms in parallel
choice_d: The model supports multiple programming languages for code generation
explanation: Multimodal AI models can understand and generate multiple data modalities — text, images, audio, video, and code. Gemini is natively multimodal, enabling it to reason across text and images in the same context window.

stem: Which generative AI use case would best help a customer service team reduce response time?
choice_a: Using gen AI to generate synthetic training data for computer vision
choice_b: Deploying a conversational AI chatbot that answers common customer queries automatically
choice_c: Training a classification model to sort incoming emails by topic
choice_d: Using gen AI to optimize database query performance
explanation: A conversational AI chatbot can handle common, repetitive customer queries automatically and 24/7, significantly reducing response time and freeing human agents for complex issues. This is a direct, high-impact application of generative AI for customer service.

stem: A marketing team wants to generate multiple variations of ad copy quickly. Which generative AI capability is most relevant?
choice_a: Anomaly detection
choice_b: Text generation and content creation
choice_c: Predictive analytics
choice_d: Computer vision object detection
explanation: Text generation is a core generative AI capability that can produce multiple variations of marketing copy, slogans, and ad content quickly. This dramatically accelerates creative workflows by providing diverse options for human review and selection.

stem: What is 'hallucination' in large language models?
choice_a: When the model generates content with perfect accuracy
choice_b: When the model confidently produces factually incorrect or fabricated information
choice_c: When the model refuses to answer a question
choice_d: When the model generates content in the wrong language
explanation: Hallucination refers to LLMs generating plausible-sounding but factually incorrect, nonsensical, or fabricated information with apparent confidence. It's a significant reliability concern. Techniques like grounding, RAG, and human review help mitigate hallucinations.

stem: Which principle of responsible AI ensures that AI decisions can be understood and justified?
choice_a: Scalability
choice_b: Explainability
choice_c: Latency optimization
choice_d: Cost efficiency
explanation: Explainability means that AI system decisions and predictions can be understood by humans. It's a core responsible AI principle enabling trust, accountability, and the ability to identify and correct issues like bias or errors in model behavior.

stem: What is 'algorithmic bias' in AI systems?
choice_a: When an algorithm consistently runs slower than expected
choice_b: When systematic errors in a model produce unfair outcomes that favor certain groups over others
choice_c: When a model uses too much computational power
choice_d: When an algorithm generates biased random numbers
explanation: Algorithmic bias occurs when AI systems produce systematically unfair outcomes, often because training data reflects historical inequalities or doesn't adequately represent all groups. It can perpetuate or amplify discrimination in high-stakes decisions.

stem: What does the BLEU score measure in generative AI evaluation?
choice_a: The speed of model inference
choice_b: The quality of machine-generated text by comparing it to reference translations using n-gram overlap
choice_c: The safety of model outputs
choice_d: The cost of running a model in production
explanation: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures how much a generated text overlaps with one or more reference texts using n-gram precision. Originally designed for machine translation, it's widely used to evaluate text generation quality.

stem: What does 'perplexity' measure in language model evaluation?
choice_a: How confused the user is by the model's output
choice_b: How well the model predicts a sample of text — lower perplexity indicates better predictive performance
choice_c: The number of parameters in the model
choice_d: The latency of model responses
explanation: Perplexity measures how well a probability model predicts a test sample. A lower perplexity score indicates the model is better at predicting the sequence. It's commonly used to compare language models during training and evaluation.

stem: Which Gemini model variant is optimized for on-device, low-latency tasks?
choice_a: Gemini Ultra
choice_b: Gemini Pro
choice_c: Gemini Flash
choice_d: Gemini Nano
explanation: Gemini Nano is Google's smallest, most efficient model designed for on-device tasks on mobile and edge devices. It offers low latency and can run without network connectivity, making it ideal for real-time, privacy-preserving applications.

stem: What makes Gemini unique compared to many other large language models?
choice_a: Gemini is only available for Google employees
choice_b: Gemini was built natively multimodal, able to reason across text, images, audio, video, and code from the ground up
choice_c: Gemini only processes text and cannot handle images
choice_d: Gemini is the only LLM that supports fine-tuning
explanation: Unlike models that add multimodal capabilities as extensions, Gemini was designed from the ground up to be natively multimodal. It can seamlessly reason about and generate text, images, audio, video, and code within a single model.

stem: Which Gemini model is best suited for complex reasoning tasks requiring maximum capability?
choice_a: Gemini Nano
choice_b: Gemini Flash
choice_c: Gemini Ultra
choice_d: Gemini Lite
explanation: Gemini Ultra is Google's most capable model, designed for highly complex tasks requiring sophisticated reasoning, coding, and multimodal understanding. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on demanding benchmarks.

stem: What is Vertex AI's primary purpose on Google Cloud?
choice_a: Vertex AI is a networking service for connecting GCP resources
choice_b: Vertex AI is a unified platform for building, deploying, and managing machine learning models at scale
choice_c: Vertex AI is Google's primary database service for AI applications
choice_d: Vertex AI is exclusively used for Gemini model inference
explanation: Vertex AI is Google Cloud's unified ML platform that brings together tools for the entire ML lifecycle: data preparation, model training, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring. It supports both Google's foundation models and custom models.

stem: What feature of Vertex AI helps developers quickly prototype and test generative AI prompts?
choice_a: Vertex AI Workbench
choice_b: Generative AI Studio
choice_c: Vertex AI Pipelines
choice_d: Vertex AI Matching Engine
explanation: Generative AI Studio within Vertex AI provides a no-code interface for testing, tuning, and deploying generative AI models. Developers can design prompts, adjust parameters like temperature, and test model responses without writing code.

stem: What is the Vertex AI Matching Engine used for?
choice_a: Matching job candidates to open positions using AI
choice_b: High-scale, low-latency vector similarity search for nearest-neighbor retrieval
choice_c: Matching database schemas for migration projects
choice_d: A/B testing different model versions in production
explanation: Vertex AI Matching Engine (now part of Vector Search) provides high-scale, low-latency approximate nearest neighbor search over high-dimensional vector embeddings. It's essential infrastructure for RAG systems and semantic search applications.

stem: What is the Model Garden in Vertex AI?
choice_a: A physical data center where Google stores its AI models
choice_b: A curated catalog of foundation models from Google and third-party providers that can be discovered, tested, and deployed
choice_c: A version control system for ML model artifacts
choice_d: A monitoring dashboard for deployed models
explanation: The Model Garden is a centralized model hub in Vertex AI where users can discover, test, and deploy a wide range of foundation models including Google's Gemini, third-party open models (Llama, Mistral), and specialized models for various tasks.

stem: What is Duet AI / Gemini for Google Workspace designed to do?
choice_a: Replace all human workers in an organization with AI
choice_b: Enhance productivity by integrating gen AI capabilities into Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Meet
choice_c: Provide cloud infrastructure management through AI
choice_d: Generate training data for custom ML models
explanation: Duet AI (now rebranded as Gemini for Google Workspace) integrates generative AI into productivity tools like Gmail (drafting emails), Docs (writing assistance), Sheets (formula generation), Slides (content creation), and Meet (notes and summaries).

stem: Which Gemini for Workspace feature helps users during video meetings?
choice_a: Automatic code review for software engineers
choice_b: AI-generated meeting notes, action items, and summaries in Google Meet
choice_c: Automatic scheduling of follow-up meetings
choice_d: Real-time translation of all spoken languages
explanation: Gemini in Google Meet can automatically generate meeting notes, capture action items, and create summaries of recorded meetings, helping participants stay focused on discussions rather than note-taking and improving post-meeting productivity.

stem: What is Google's Secure AI Framework (SAIF)?
choice_a: A hardware security module for encrypting AI model weights
choice_b: A conceptual framework by Google for making AI systems more secure, covering six core elements of AI security
choice_c: A compliance certification required to use Google Cloud AI services
choice_d: A firewall service specifically designed for AI workloads
explanation: SAIF (Secure AI Framework) is Google's open framework for AI security best practices. It covers six elements: expanding strong security foundations to AI, extending detection/response to include AI-specific risks, automating defenses, harmonizing platform-level controls, adapting controls to adjust mitigations, and contextualizing AI risk in existing processes.

stem: How does Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) benefit organizations using Google Cloud AI?
choice_a: CMEK automatically reduces the cost of AI model training
choice_b: CMEK gives organizations control over the encryption keys used to protect their data, meeting compliance requirements
choice_c: CMEK speeds up model inference by using hardware encryption
choice_d: CMEK prevents models from generating harmful content
explanation: CMEK lets organizations use their own encryption keys (stored in Cloud Key Management Service or external HSMs) to encrypt data at rest in Google Cloud services. This gives organizations greater control over data security and helps meet regulatory compliance requirements.

stem: What is Dialogflow CX used for on Google Cloud?
choice_a: Building and deploying virtual agents with complex conversation flows using state machines
choice_b: Creating static FAQ pages for websites
choice_c: Monitoring AI model performance in production
choice_d: Managing large-scale batch AI inference jobs
explanation: Dialogflow CX is Google Cloud's advanced conversational AI platform for building enterprise-grade virtual agents and chatbots. It uses a flow-based (state machine) design for complex, multi-turn conversations across multiple channels.

stem: What is Google Agentspace?
choice_a: A physical office space provided by Google for AI startups
choice_b: An enterprise AI platform that helps employees access information and take action across business systems using AI agents
choice_c: A tool for managing Kubernetes deployments of AI models
choice_d: A Google Cloud service for storing and versioning AI model artifacts
explanation: Google Agentspace is an enterprise AI platform that provides employees with an intelligent assistant that can search across enterprise data sources, answer questions, and take actions across business systems like ServiceNow, Jira, and Salesforce using AI agents.

stem: What is 'few-shot prompting' in prompt engineering?
choice_a: Providing the model with no examples and asking it to generalize
choice_b: Providing a small number of example input-output pairs in the prompt to guide the model's behavior
choice_c: Fine-tuning a model on a small dataset
choice_d: Using a shorter prompt to reduce token costs
explanation: Few-shot prompting includes a small number of demonstrations (input-output examples) in the prompt itself, teaching the model the desired format and behavior without any model weight updates. It's contrasted with zero-shot (no examples) and many-shot prompting.

stem: What is 'chain-of-thought' prompting?
choice_a: Linking multiple AI models together in a pipeline
choice_b: A prompting technique that encourages the model to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer
choice_c: Chaining multiple API calls in a sequence
choice_d: A method of compressing long prompts into shorter ones
explanation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting instructs or demonstrates to the model that it should show its reasoning process step-by-step before giving the final answer. This improves performance on complex reasoning, math, and multi-step problems.

stem: What does the 'temperature' parameter control in a generative AI model?
choice_a: The physical temperature of the GPU during inference
choice_b: The randomness/creativity of model outputs — higher temperature produces more varied responses
choice_c: The speed of model inference
choice_d: The maximum number of tokens the model can generate
explanation: Temperature controls the randomness of token sampling during generation. Low temperature (near 0) makes outputs more deterministic and focused. High temperature produces more diverse and creative, but potentially less coherent outputs. It's a key parameter for controlling generation style.

stem: What is 'system prompting' (or system instructions) in generative AI?
choice_a: Prompts sent by the operating system to control model execution
choice_b: Persistent instructions provided to a model that define its role, persona, and behavioral constraints across all interactions
choice_c: A technique for optimizing GPU utilization during training
choice_d: The default prompt used when no user input is provided
explanation: System prompts (or system instructions) are persistent instructions provided to a model before the user conversation begins. They define the model's role (e.g., 'You are a helpful customer support agent'), persona, output format, and behavioral boundaries.

stem: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?
choice_a: A technique for generating synthetic training data for model fine-tuning
choice_b: A technique that retrieves relevant information from external knowledge sources at inference time to ground model responses
choice_c: A method for training models on retrieved internet data
choice_d: A Google Cloud service for archiving generated content
explanation: RAG combines a retrieval system (e.g., vector search over a document store) with a generative model. At inference time, relevant documents are retrieved and included in the prompt as context. This grounds the model's response in factual, up-to-date information, reducing hallucinations.

stem: What problem does 'grounding' primarily solve in generative AI?
choice_a: Improving model training speed
choice_b: Reducing hallucinations by connecting model outputs to verifiable, real-world information
choice_c: Lowering the cost of model deployment
choice_d: Enabling models to process longer context windows
explanation: Grounding connects a model's outputs to authoritative, real-world information sources (documents, databases, search results), reducing the risk of hallucinations. Grounded models cite their sources and stay factually anchored to provided data rather than relying solely on parametric knowledge.

stem: In a RAG system, what role does the vector database play?
choice_a: It stores the model weights for fast retrieval
choice_b: It stores document embeddings and enables fast semantic similarity search to find relevant context
choice_c: It tracks user queries for analytics purposes
choice_d: It encrypts generated content before sending to users
explanation: In RAG, a vector database (like Vertex AI Vector Search or Pinecone) stores embeddings of document chunks. At query time, the user's query is embedded and compared against stored embeddings to retrieve the most semantically relevant documents, which are then included as context for the LLM.

stem: What is 'fine-tuning' in the context of foundation models?
choice_a: Adjusting the hardware configuration for faster model inference
choice_b: Further training a pre-trained foundation model on domain-specific or task-specific data to improve performance on targeted tasks
choice_c: Compressing a model to reduce its size for deployment
choice_d: Evaluating a model against a benchmark dataset
explanation: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained foundation model to specific domains or tasks by continuing training on a smaller, task-specific dataset. It updates some or all model weights to specialize behavior, improving performance on tasks not well-represented in the original pre-training data.

stem: What does RLHF stand for and why is it important?
choice_a: Rapid Learning with High Frequency — a technique for speeding up model training
choice_b: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — aligning model behavior with human preferences and values
choice_c: Retrieval Learning with Hierarchical Fine-tuning — a type of RAG optimization
choice_d: Robust Language Handling Framework — a safety evaluation standard
explanation: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) trains models to align with human preferences by having human raters evaluate model outputs, then using these ratings to train a reward model, which guides further RL training. It's key to making LLMs helpful, harmless, and honest.

stem: What is the advantage of LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for fine-tuning?
choice_a: LoRA eliminates the need for any training data
choice_b: LoRA reduces fine-tuning costs by training only small adapter matrices rather than all model weights
choice_c: LoRA automatically selects the best hyperparameters for fine-tuning
choice_d: LoRA improves model performance without any additional training
explanation: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) technique that inserts small trainable matrices (adapters) into transformer layers while keeping original weights frozen. This dramatically reduces memory and compute requirements while achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning.

stem: What is a common source of bias in AI training data?
choice_a: Using too many programming languages in the training corpus
choice_b: Historical data that reflects societal inequalities, underrepresentation of certain groups, or collection methods that skew samples
choice_c: Training on data from multiple geographic regions
choice_d: Including data from different time periods
explanation: Training data bias often stems from historical data encoding societal inequalities, over- or under-representation of demographic groups, geographic and linguistic imbalances, and biased data collection methods. These biases can be amplified by AI systems, producing discriminatory outputs.

stem: What is 'model evaluation' in the context of responsible AI?
choice_a: Measuring the cost and latency of model deployment
choice_b: Systematically assessing model performance, fairness, safety, and potential harms before and after deployment
choice_c: Comparing different cloud providers for AI services
choice_d: Reviewing the source code of a model for vulnerabilities
explanation: Responsible AI model evaluation goes beyond accuracy metrics to assess fairness across demographic groups, safety (harmful output rates), robustness to adversarial inputs, and alignment with intended use. This comprehensive evaluation helps prevent harm before deployment and monitors ongoing behavior.

stem: What is the most important first step when building an organizational AI strategy?
choice_a: Immediately deploying AI in all business processes
choice_b: Identifying clear business problems and opportunities where AI can deliver measurable value
choice_c: Hiring a large team of AI researchers
choice_d: Purchasing the most expensive AI hardware available
explanation: Effective AI strategy starts with clearly identifying business challenges and opportunities where AI can create measurable value. Without this foundation, AI initiatives risk being technology-driven rather than value-driven, leading to wasted investment and failed projects.

stem: What does 'AI-first culture' mean in an organization?
choice_a: Only hiring employees with AI expertise
choice_b: A mindset where AI is considered as a strategic tool for every business decision and process improvement
choice_c: Replacing all human workers with AI systems
choice_d: Using only AI-generated content in all communications
explanation: An AI-first culture means that leaders and employees consistently consider how AI can improve decisions, products, and processes. It requires upskilling, fostering experimentation, creating cross-functional collaboration between business and technical teams, and integrating AI into strategic planning.

stem: What is a key metric for measuring the success of a generative AI implementation?
choice_a: Number of AI models deployed
choice_b: Business outcomes such as productivity gains, cost reduction, revenue impact, or customer satisfaction improvement
choice_c: Lines of code written to implement the AI solution
choice_d: Amount of data ingested by the AI system
explanation: Successful AI implementations are measured by their impact on business outcomes: productivity improvements (time saved), cost reduction, revenue generation, customer satisfaction scores (NPS/CSAT), and quality improvements. Technical metrics like model accuracy are intermediate, not final measures of success.

stem: What is the concept of 'human-in-the-loop' in AI deployment?
choice_a: Requiring humans to manually type all inputs to AI systems
choice_b: Designing AI systems so humans review, validate, or override AI decisions at critical points
choice_c: Using human-generated synthetic data for model training
choice_d: Monitoring AI systems' physical hardware performance
explanation: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) systems incorporate human judgment at critical decision points — humans review AI outputs before they're acted upon, especially for high-stakes decisions. This provides a safety net, improves accuracy, and maintains accountability when AI systems might make consequential errors.

stem: According to Google's AI principles, what is a key commitment regarding AI development?
choice_a: AI should only be developed for military applications
choice_b: AI should be socially beneficial, avoid creating unfair bias, be built and tested for safety, and be accountable to people
choice_c: AI development should prioritize speed over safety
choice_d: AI should operate without any human oversight
explanation: Google's AI Principles commit to building AI that is socially beneficial, avoids unfair bias, is built and tested for safety, is accountable to people, incorporates privacy design, upholds scientific excellence, and is made available for uses consistent with these principles.

stem: What is 'data privacy' in the context of AI systems?
choice_a: Keeping AI model weights confidential from competitors
choice_b: Ensuring that personal information used to train or process AI models is collected, stored, and used appropriately and in compliance with regulations
choice_c: Encrypting all AI-generated outputs
choice_d: Preventing users from accessing AI systems
explanation: Data privacy in AI involves ensuring that personal data used in AI systems is handled lawfully (consent, purpose limitation), stored securely, minimized to what's necessary, and protected from unauthorized access — complying with regulations like GDPR and CCPA.

stem: Which healthcare application is a compelling use case for generative AI?
choice_a: Replacing physicians in surgical procedures
choice_b: Summarizing patient medical records and research papers to support clinical decision-making
choice_c: Automatically prescribing medications without physician approval
choice_d: Managing hospital parking reservations
explanation: In healthcare, gen AI can summarize lengthy patient records, extract key information from clinical notes, and synthesize medical research to support (not replace) physician decision-making. This helps clinicians access relevant information faster, improving care quality.

stem: How can generative AI benefit the financial services industry?
choice_a: By automatically making all investment decisions without human review
choice_b: By automating document analysis (contracts, reports), generating personalized financial advice summaries, and detecting anomalies in transaction data
choice_c: By eliminating all regulatory compliance requirements
choice_d: By replacing human financial advisors entirely
explanation: Financial services gen AI applications include: intelligent document processing (contracts, regulatory filings), personalized customer communication, fraud pattern explanation, investment research summarization, and risk report generation — all augmenting human analysts rather than replacing them.

stem: What is the purpose of Vertex AI Model Monitoring?
choice_a: To monitor GPU temperature during model training
choice_b: To detect data drift and model drift in deployed models, alerting teams when model performance degrades
choice_c: To monitor the cost of Vertex AI usage
choice_d: To track which users are accessing the model
explanation: Vertex AI Model Monitoring tracks deployed model performance over time, detecting when the statistical distribution of input data drifts from training data (data drift) or when prediction quality degrades (model drift). Alerts allow teams to retrain or update models before significant business impact occurs.

stem: What is a 'context window' in large language models?
choice_a: The browser window used to access the AI interface
choice_b: The maximum amount of text (tokens) a model can consider at once when generating a response
choice_c: The geographic region where the model is deployed
choice_d: The time period during which a model was trained
explanation: The context window defines how much text (measured in tokens) an LLM can process simultaneously. It includes the system prompt, conversation history, documents, and the current query. Larger context windows (e.g., Gemini 1.5's 1M tokens) allow models to reason over entire books or codebases at once.

stem: What is 'prompt engineering' in Generative AI Studio?
choice_a: Building physical hardware for running AI models
choice_b: The practice of crafting, testing, and refining input instructions to get optimal outputs from generative AI models
choice_c: Engineering the source code of a generative AI model
choice_d: Monitoring model prompts for security vulnerabilities
explanation: Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing effective inputs to generative AI models. Generative AI Studio provides a sandbox environment for experimenting with different prompt structures, few-shot examples, system instructions, and parameters to optimize model outputs.

stem: What is 'zero-shot prompting'?
choice_a: Prompting a model that has received zero training
choice_b: Asking a model to perform a task without providing any examples, relying on the model's pre-trained knowledge
choice_c: Using a prompt with zero tokens
choice_d: A technique that uses zero temperature for deterministic outputs
explanation: Zero-shot prompting asks a model to perform a task purely based on its pre-trained knowledge, without providing any task examples. The model must generalize from its training. Effective for capable models but may require few-shot examples for specialized or complex tasks.

stem: Which of the following is NOT a type of machine learning?
choice_a: Supervised learning
choice_b: Unsupervised learning
choice_c: Deterministic learning
choice_d: Reinforcement learning
explanation: The three main types of machine learning are: supervised learning (labeled data), unsupervised learning (unlabeled data), and reinforcement learning (reward-based). 'Deterministic learning' is not a recognized category — deterministic systems don't learn, they follow fixed rules.

stem: What is the purpose of Data Loss Prevention (DLP) in the context of AI on Google Cloud?
choice_a: Preventing physical data center outages
choice_b: Discovering, classifying, and protecting sensitive information in data used by AI systems
choice_c: Compressing AI model outputs to reduce storage costs
choice_d: Backing up AI model weights to prevent data loss
explanation: Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) helps organizations identify, classify, and protect sensitive information (PII, financial data, health records) in datasets used for AI training and inference. It ensures sensitive data doesn't inadvertently flow into AI systems or appear in AI outputs.

stem: What is a key challenge in change management when implementing AI in an organization?
choice_a: Choosing the right cloud provider
choice_b: Addressing employee fear of job displacement and building trust through transparent communication and upskilling
choice_c: Finding enough GPU compute for training
choice_d: Selecting the right programming language for AI development
explanation: A primary change management challenge is addressing employee concerns about job displacement. Successful AI adoption requires transparent communication about AI's role (augmentation vs. replacement), visible leadership support, and upskilling programs that help employees work alongside AI effectively.

stem: What is 'prompt injection' and why is it a security concern?
choice_a: Adding too many examples to a few-shot prompt
choice_b: An attack where malicious instructions are embedded in user inputs to hijack an AI model's behavior
choice_c: Injecting training data into a deployed model
choice_d: A technique for speeding up prompt processing
explanation: Prompt injection is an attack where adversarial instructions are hidden in inputs (e.g., documents, web pages, user messages) to override a model's system instructions and cause unintended behavior. It's analogous to SQL injection — a serious security concern for AI applications processing untrusted content.

stem: What is Gemini Flash optimized for compared to Gemini Pro?
choice_a: Maximum reasoning capability and accuracy
choice_b: Speed and efficiency for high-volume, latency-sensitive tasks at lower cost
choice_c: On-device processing without internet connectivity
choice_d: Exclusively processing image data
explanation: Gemini Flash is optimized for speed and cost efficiency, making it ideal for high-throughput, latency-sensitive applications where response time and cost matter more than maximum capability. It's Google's fast, efficient model for production workloads at scale.

stem: What is 'semantic search' and how does it differ from keyword search?
choice_a: Semantic search is faster but less accurate than keyword search
choice_b: Semantic search understands the meaning and intent of queries rather than matching exact keywords, returning conceptually relevant results
choice_c: Semantic search only works with structured data
choice_d: Semantic search requires more user input to function
explanation: Unlike keyword search (exact string matching), semantic search uses embeddings to understand the meaning and intent behind queries. A semantic search for 'ways to save money' will find documents about 'frugal living tips' even without keyword overlap. This is foundational to RAG systems.

stem: What is an 'AI proof of concept' (PoC) and when should it be used?
choice_a: A legal document proving that an AI system doesn't infringe on patents
choice_b: A small-scale prototype that validates whether AI can solve a specific problem before committing to full implementation
choice_c: A certification that proves an AI model meets safety standards
choice_d: A government regulation requiring proof that AI systems work as intended
explanation: An AI PoC is a small, focused prototype that tests whether AI can viably address a specific business problem. PoCs reduce risk by validating technical feasibility, data quality, and business value before significant investment. They help organizations learn fast and fail cheaply.

stem: How can generative AI benefit the retail industry?
choice_a: By replacing physical retail stores entirely
choice_b: By personalizing product recommendations, generating tailored marketing content, powering shopping assistants, and automating product descriptions
choice_c: By managing inventory through physical robots exclusively
choice_d: By eliminating the need for human customer service representatives
explanation: Retail gen AI applications include: hyper-personalized product recommendations, AI shopping assistants, automated product description generation, personalized email/ad copy, virtual try-ons, and customer service chatbots. These applications drive conversion, reduce costs, and improve customer experience.

stem: What is 'transfer learning' in machine learning?
choice_a: Moving data between different cloud storage buckets
choice_b: Applying knowledge gained from training on one task to improve performance on a related but different task
choice_c: Transferring a model from one cloud provider to another
choice_d: Sending model predictions to downstream systems
explanation: Transfer learning leverages knowledge from pre-trained models, adapting them to new tasks with less data and compute. Foundation models are the ultimate form of transfer learning — pre-trained on vast data, then fine-tuned for specific tasks. This is why foundation models are transformative.

stem: What is the benefit of using Vertex AI's managed notebooks (Colab Enterprise)?
choice_a: They provide free unlimited compute for any AI workload
choice_b: They offer managed, scalable notebook environments with built-in security, collaboration, and direct access to GCP services
choice_c: They automatically write code for any ML task without user input
choice_d: They replace the need for any data science expertise
explanation: Vertex AI's managed notebooks provide secure, scalable Jupyter environments with GCP integration (BigQuery, Vertex AI services), team collaboration features, and auto-scaling compute. They eliminate infrastructure management overhead so data scientists can focus on model development.

stem: When is fine-tuning preferred over prompt engineering for improving model performance?
choice_a: When you want to quickly test a new capability without significant investment
choice_b: When you have substantial domain-specific training data, need consistent behavior at scale, or require capabilities not achievable through prompting alone
choice_c: When the task is simple enough that basic prompts already work well
choice_d: When you don't have any labeled data available
explanation: Fine-tuning is preferred when: you have sufficient high-quality domain-specific data (hundreds to thousands of examples), you need consistent style/format at scale (not relying on lengthy prompts), or the task requires knowledge/behavior not captured in the base model. It has higher upfront cost but lower inference cost.

stem: What is 'model transparency' in responsible AI?
choice_a: Making the model's source code publicly available
choice_b: Providing clear information about how AI models work, their limitations, training data, and decision-making processes
choice_c: Ensuring model outputs are always visible to all users
choice_d: Removing all security restrictions from AI systems
explanation: Model transparency means being open about how AI systems make decisions, what data they were trained on, their known limitations, and how they should and shouldn't be used. It builds trust, enables appropriate oversight, and helps users understand when to question AI outputs.

stem: What is 'code generation' as a generative AI use case?
choice_a: Generating QR codes for authentication
choice_b: Using AI models to automatically write, complete, explain, debug, or translate programming code
choice_c: Generating machine code from high-level programming languages
choice_d: Creating security codes for encryption systems
explanation: Code generation is a powerful gen AI use case where models write, complete, explain, refactor, or debug code from natural language descriptions or partial code. Tools like GitHub Copilot and Gemini Code Assist can significantly accelerate developer productivity.

stem: What is a 'multi-agent system' in generative AI?
choice_a: A system with multiple human employees reviewing AI outputs
choice_b: A system where multiple specialized AI agents collaborate, each handling different aspects of a complex task
choice_c: Multiple copies of the same AI model running in parallel for redundancy
choice_d: A system requiring multiple API keys to function
explanation: Multi-agent systems use multiple specialized AI agents that collaborate to complete complex tasks. One orchestrator agent breaks down tasks and delegates to specialist agents (e.g., a research agent, code agent, data agent). This enables tackling problems too complex for a single model.

stem: What does FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) measure in generative AI evaluation?
choice_a: The financial impact of AI deployment
choice_b: The quality and diversity of generated images by comparing their feature distributions to real images
choice_c: The inference speed of image generation models
choice_d: The number of distinct objects that can be generated by an image model
explanation: FID measures image generation quality by comparing the statistical distribution of features in generated images versus real images using a pre-trained inception network. Lower FID scores indicate higher quality and more realistic generated images.

stem: What is NotebookLM and what is its primary purpose?
choice_a: A machine learning training notebook on Google Cloud
choice_b: An AI-powered research tool by Google that grounds responses in user-uploaded documents to help understand and synthesize complex information
choice_c: A version control system for Jupyter notebooks
choice_d: A Google Cloud monitoring tool for LLM performance
explanation: NotebookLM is Google's AI-powered research assistant that analyzes user-uploaded documents (PDFs, Google Docs, etc.) and answers questions grounded in those sources. It can create summaries, briefing docs, and even audio overviews, making it a powerful tool for researchers and knowledge workers.

stem: What is 'top-k sampling' in language model generation?
choice_a: Selecting the top-k most important features for training
choice_b: Restricting token sampling to the k most probable next tokens, controlling generation randomness
choice_c: Evaluating a model on the top-k benchmark datasets
choice_d: Using the top-k training examples for few-shot learning
explanation: Top-k sampling restricts the sampling pool to the k most probable next tokens at each generation step, then samples from this truncated distribution. Setting k=1 (greedy decoding) gives the most likely token; larger k values allow more diversity. It helps avoid very low-probability tokens.

stem: What is 'data governance' in the context of AI projects?
choice_a: Government regulations that prohibit AI use in certain industries
choice_b: The framework of policies, processes, and standards ensuring data quality, security, privacy, and appropriate use throughout the AI lifecycle
choice_c: A committee that approves which datasets can be purchased
choice_d: The technical process of migrating data between databases
explanation: Data governance for AI establishes policies for data collection (consent, sourcing), quality (validation, labeling standards), security (access control, encryption), privacy (PII handling, anonymization), and lineage (tracking data origin and transformations). Without strong data governance, AI projects face compliance, quality, and trust risks.

stem: What is 'tokenization' in large language models?
choice_a: Converting AI model outputs to financial tokens for payment
choice_b: The process of breaking text into smaller units (tokens) that the model processes — tokens are typically words, subwords, or characters
choice_c: Generating unique security tokens for API authentication
choice_d: Converting binary model weights into readable text
explanation: Tokenization converts input text into tokens — the basic units LLMs process. Tokens are typically subword units (e.g., BPE encoding) that balance vocabulary size and coverage. Understanding tokenization matters for pricing (charged per token), context window limits, and understanding model behavior with different languages.

stem: What does 'model governance' mean in enterprise AI deployments?
choice_a: Choosing which government regulations apply to your AI system
choice_b: Policies and processes for managing the AI model lifecycle including versioning, access control, audit trails, and retirement
choice_c: A governance body that decides which AI models to purchase
choice_d: Ensuring models are stored in a governance-compliant data center
explanation: Model governance encompasses the policies and processes for managing AI models throughout their lifecycle: version control, access permissions (who can deploy/modify models), audit trails of model changes and decisions, performance monitoring, and model retirement criteria. It's essential for enterprise accountability and compliance.

stem: How can generative AI transform the education sector?
choice_a: By replacing all teachers with AI tutors
choice_b: By creating personalized learning experiences, generating custom practice materials, providing instant feedback, and supporting diverse learning needs
choice_c: By automatically grading all examinations without teacher review
choice_d: By standardizing all curricula globally
explanation: Gen AI in education enables: personalized tutoring adapted to individual learning pace, automatically generated practice problems and explanations, instant feedback on writing, accessibility tools for diverse learners, and support for teachers in creating lesson materials — augmenting rather than replacing educators.

stem: What is 'model watermarking' used for in generative AI?
choice_a: Adding visual watermarks to AI-generated images for copyright protection and attribution
choice_b: Both A and the ability to detect AI-generated content to prevent misuse and misinformation
choice_c: Compressing model weights to reduce storage costs
choice_d: Marking training data for quality control purposes
explanation: Model watermarking techniques embed invisible signals in AI-generated content (images, text, audio) to identify it as AI-generated. This serves copyright protection (content attribution) and misinformation prevention (detecting AI-generated deepfakes or disinformation). Google's SynthID is an example.

stem: What is a 'safety filter' in the context of generative AI?
choice_a: A hardware component that protects AI GPUs from power surges
choice_b: A system that evaluates and blocks model outputs containing harmful, offensive, or dangerous content before they reach users
choice_c: A network firewall that protects AI APIs from unauthorized access
choice_d: A data filter that removes sensitive information from training datasets
explanation: Safety filters are content moderation systems applied to AI model outputs. They evaluate generated content against safety policies, blocking or flagging outputs that contain hate speech, violence, sexual content, dangerous instructions, or other policy violations before delivery to users.

stem: What is the purpose of 'model versioning' in Vertex AI?
choice_a: Displaying the version number in the model's API responses
choice_b: Maintaining multiple versions of a model to enable A/B testing, rollback, and tracking improvements over time
choice_c: Renaming models when they are retrained
choice_d: Automatically updating models to the latest version without user intervention
explanation: Model versioning in Vertex AI allows teams to maintain, compare, and deploy multiple versions of models. This enables A/B testing between versions, safe deployment with the ability to roll back to a previous version if issues arise, and tracking model lineage and performance improvements over time.

stem: What is a 'Center of Excellence' (CoE) for AI in an organization?
choice_a: A data center dedicated exclusively to AI computing
choice_b: A centralized team that drives AI strategy, establishes standards, builds reusable capabilities, and supports business units in AI adoption
choice_c: A competitive assessment of AI capabilities relative to industry peers
choice_d: An external consulting firm hired to implement AI projects
explanation: An AI Center of Excellence (CoE) is a cross-functional team that centralizes AI expertise and governance. It sets standards and best practices, builds reusable AI components, provides guidance to business units, manages AI tools and platforms, and tracks AI initiative performance across the organization.

stem: What is 'text summarization' as a generative AI application?
choice_a: Replacing long words with shorter synonyms
choice_b: Automatically generating concise, coherent summaries of longer documents, articles, or conversations
choice_c: Converting spoken audio to written text
choice_d: Translating text between languages
explanation: Text summarization uses generative AI to produce concise summaries of longer content — research papers, news articles, meeting transcripts, legal documents, or customer feedback. It can be extractive (selecting key sentences) or abstractive (generating new summary text), dramatically reducing information processing time.

stem: What is the 'top-p' (nucleus) sampling parameter in language generation?
choice_a: The probability that the model will accept or reject a user prompt
choice_b: A sampling method that considers the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p, controlling output diversity
choice_c: The percentage of training data to use for validation
choice_d: The top percentage of users who can access a model
explanation: Top-p (nucleus) sampling selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability mass exceeds p, then samples from this set. Unlike top-k (fixed number of tokens), top-p adapts to the probability distribution — using fewer tokens when one is highly probable and more when probabilities are spread evenly.

stem: What is Google's AI Hypercomputer and what does it enable?
choice_a: A physical supercomputer available for rent by AI researchers
choice_b: A tightly integrated system of AI-optimized hardware (TPUs, GPUs), networking, and software optimized for large-scale AI training and inference at high efficiency
choice_c: A consumer laptop optimized for running AI applications locally
choice_d: An AI system that manages Google's cloud infrastructure
explanation: Google's AI Hypercomputer is an integrated AI supercomputing architecture combining custom TPUs/GPUs, ultra-high-bandwidth networking (ICI), and optimized system software. It enables efficient large-scale model training and serving, underpinning Google's AI infrastructure for training Gemini and serving AI workloads.

stem: What does 'responsible AI' adoption require from business leaders?
choice_a: Minimizing AI investment to reduce risk exposure
choice_b: Establishing clear AI governance policies, ensuring diverse teams build AI, auditing for bias, and maintaining accountability for AI decisions
choice_c: Delegating all AI decisions to technical teams without executive involvement
choice_d: Avoiding AI use until all regulatory frameworks are finalized
explanation: Responsible AI leadership requires: defining ethical guidelines and governance structures, ensuring diverse, inclusive teams develop AI systems, regularly auditing for bias and unintended consequences, establishing clear accountability chains for AI decisions, and engaging openly with stakeholders about AI use.

stem: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
choice_a: Supervised learning requires a teacher, while unsupervised learning uses self-study materials
choice_b: Supervised learning trains on labeled data with known outputs; unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data without predefined answers
choice_c: Supervised learning is more accurate than unsupervised learning in all cases
choice_d: Unsupervised learning requires more compute than supervised learning
explanation: Supervised learning uses labeled training data (input-output pairs) to learn mappings, enabling tasks like classification and regression. Unsupervised learning finds hidden patterns (clusters, dimensions) in unlabeled data. Both are fundamental ML paradigms with different use cases.

stem: What distinguishes Dialogflow CX from Dialogflow ES?
choice_a: Dialogflow CX is cheaper but less capable
choice_b: Dialogflow CX uses a flow-based (state machine) design for complex multi-turn conversations, while ES uses a simpler intent-based model
choice_c: Dialogflow ES supports more languages than CX
choice_d: Dialogflow CX only works for voice applications
explanation: Dialogflow CX uses a visual flow/page-based model suitable for complex enterprise chatbots with multiple conversation paths. Dialogflow ES (Essentials) uses a simpler intent-entity model suitable for smaller chatbots. CX offers better scalability, versioning, and testing for enterprise deployments.

stem: What is 'chunking' in the context of RAG systems?
choice_a: Dividing computing tasks across multiple GPUs
choice_b: Breaking documents into smaller segments before embedding them, optimizing relevance and context in retrieval
choice_c: Compressing model weights into chunks for efficient storage
choice_d: Grouping similar user queries for batch processing
explanation: Chunking splits documents into smaller pieces (by paragraph, sentence, or fixed token count) before creating embeddings for a vector database. Good chunking strategy is crucial — too large and retrieved chunks lose specificity; too small and they lose context. Chunk size and overlap are important hyperparameters for RAG quality.

stem: What manufacturing use case benefits from generative AI?
choice_a: Physically assembling products on the factory floor
choice_b: Generating maintenance reports, predicting equipment failures using anomaly detection, optimizing supply chains, and assisting engineers with technical documentation
choice_c: Operating heavy machinery autonomously without human oversight
choice_d: Replacing all quality control inspections with AI
explanation: Manufacturing gen AI applications include: predictive maintenance (detecting equipment anomalies before failures), technical documentation generation, supply chain optimization through scenario analysis, quality report automation, and engineering assistant tools that help workers access technical knowledge faster.

stem: What distinguishes structured data from unstructured data?
choice_a: Structured data is always larger in volume than unstructured data.
choice_b: Structured data is organised in predefined schemas (e.g., database tables, spreadsheets), while unstructured data lacks a fixed format (e.g., emails, images, audio).
choice_c: Unstructured data can only be processed by traditional databases, while structured data requires AI.
choice_d: Structured data is generated by humans, while unstructured data is generated by machines.
explanation: Structured data fits into rows and columns with defined data types (relational databases, CSV files). Unstructured data — which makes up ~80% of enterprise data — has no predefined schema: documents, emails, images, video, and audio. Gen AI excels at extracting value from unstructured data.

stem: Why is 'data completeness' a critical quality dimension for AI training datasets?
choice_a: Complete datasets are smaller and therefore cheaper to store.
choice_b: Incomplete data leads to models that cannot learn patterns for missing scenarios, producing biased or unreliable predictions.
choice_c: Completeness ensures the dataset was collected legally.
choice_d: Complete data reduces the time needed to deploy a model to production.
explanation: Data completeness means all required fields and records are present. Missing data creates blind spots in the model — it cannot learn patterns it has never seen, leading to poor generalisation, biased outputs, and unreliable predictions for the scenarios represented by missing records.

stem: What is the key difference between labeled and unlabeled data in machine learning?
choice_a: Labeled data is newer and more accurate than unlabeled data.
choice_b: Labeled data has human-assigned annotations (e.g., 'cat'/'dog') used for supervised learning; unlabeled data has no annotations and is used in unsupervised or self-supervised learning.
choice_c: Labeled data can only be used for image classification tasks.
choice_d: Unlabeled data must be converted to labeled data before it can be stored in a database.
explanation: Labeled data pairs inputs with human-verified outputs (e.g., customer review → 'positive sentiment'), enabling supervised learning. Unlabeled data has no annotations — it's cheaper to collect at scale and used for unsupervised learning (clustering) or self-supervised pre-training as used in foundation models like Gemini.

stem: A company's AI model performs well in testing but produces wildly different results when the same question is asked in slightly different ways. Which data quality dimension is most likely lacking?
choice_a: Availability
choice_b: Cost
choice_c: Consistency
choice_d: Format
explanation: Data consistency means that the same concept is represented uniformly across the dataset. Inconsistent training data (e.g., the same entity labelled differently, or the same question answered differently) causes models to learn contradictory patterns, producing unreliable outputs for similar inputs.

stem: Which layer of the gen AI landscape provides the compute resources (TPUs, GPUs, data centres) that all other layers depend on?
choice_a: Applications
choice_b: Platforms
choice_c: Infrastructure
choice_d: Agents
explanation: The Infrastructure layer is the foundation of the gen AI stack. It provides the physical and virtual compute resources — GPUs, TPUs, data centres, networking — that train and serve AI models. Google Cloud's AI Hypercomputer and custom TPUs sit at this layer.

stem: What is the correct order of the five core layers in the gen AI landscape, from foundational to user-facing?
choice_a: Applications → Agents → Platforms → Models → Infrastructure
choice_b: Infrastructure → Models → Platforms → Agents → Applications
choice_c: Models → Infrastructure → Agents → Platforms → Applications
choice_d: Platforms → Infrastructure → Models → Applications → Agents
explanation: The gen AI stack builds upward: Infrastructure (compute) → Models (foundation models trained on that compute) → Platforms (tools to build and manage models, e.g. Vertex AI) → Agents (AI systems that take multi-step actions using models and tools) → Applications (end-user products). Each layer depends on the one below it.

stem: A business leader wants to evaluate which layer of the gen AI landscape their organisation should invest in first. They want to use Google's pre-built foundation models without training their own. Which layer is their primary focus?
choice_a: Infrastructure — buying GPUs for on-premises deployment
choice_b: Platforms — using Vertex AI to build, deploy, and manage applications on top of existing models
choice_c: Models — building a custom foundation model from scratch
choice_d: Applications — only using consumer-facing apps like Gemini.com
explanation: Most organisations start at the Platforms layer, using Google Cloud's Vertex AI to access pre-built foundation models (Gemini), build RAG pipelines, deploy agents, and manage the ML lifecycle — without needing to build infrastructure or train foundation models from scratch.

stem: What is Gemma and what makes it distinct from Gemini?
choice_a: Gemma is Google's most powerful proprietary LLM, larger than Gemini Ultra.
choice_b: Gemma is a family of lightweight, open-source models built from the same research as Gemini, designed for on-device and edge deployment with open weights.
choice_c: Gemma is a Google Cloud service for managing Gemini API keys.
choice_d: Gemma is the consumer-facing chatbot interface for Gemini models.
explanation: Gemma is Google's family of open-weight, lightweight models derived from Gemini research. Unlike Gemini (closed, proprietary, API-accessed), Gemma's weights are publicly available, making it suitable for self-hosted deployment, fine-tuning on private data, on-device use, and organisations with data sovereignty requirements.

stem: Which Google foundation model is specifically designed for text-to-image generation?
choice_a: Gemma
choice_b: Veo
choice_c: Imagen
choice_d: Gemini Nano
explanation: Imagen is Google's text-to-image diffusion model. Given a text prompt, Imagen generates high-quality, photorealistic images. It's available via Vertex AI and powers image generation capabilities in Google products. Use cases include marketing creative, product visualisation, and design iteration.

stem: A media production company wants to generate short video clips from text descriptions to prototype advertising concepts. Which Google foundation model should they use?
choice_a: Imagen
choice_b: Gemma
choice_c: Veo
choice_d: Gemini Nano
explanation: Veo is Google's video generation model, capable of creating high-quality video clips from text prompts or images. It's Google's answer for text-to-video generation, enabling use cases like marketing prototypes, creative content, and synthetic training data for computer vision.

stem: A legal firm needs a gen AI solution to analyse entire 500-page contracts in a single pass. Which foundation model selection criterion is most critical?
choice_a: Modality — the model must support audio input
choice_b: Context window — the model must support a large enough context to process the full document at once
choice_c: Fine-tuning capability — the model must be fine-tuned on legal data
choice_d: Cost — the cheapest model should always be chosen first
explanation: Context window is the critical criterion here — a model that can't fit 500 pages in its context window would need complex chunking workarounds. Gemini 1.5 Pro's 1M-token context window is purpose-built for long-document analysis. Modality, fine-tuning, and cost are secondary considerations for this specific requirement.

stem: An organisation needs a gen AI model that can process both scanned images of forms AND the text within them simultaneously. Which model selection criterion governs this requirement?
choice_a: Cost
choice_b: Fine-tuning capability
choice_c: Availability and reliability
choice_d: Modality
explanation: Modality refers to the types of input/output a model supports. A model that can process both images and text simultaneously is multimodal. Selecting a multimodal model (e.g., Gemini) is the primary criterion when the use case requires reasoning across different data types — images, text, audio, or video together.

stem: What does Google Cloud's 'open approach' to AI mean for enterprise customers?
choice_a: Google Cloud makes all its proprietary model weights publicly available.
choice_b: Enterprise customers can use open-source models (e.g., Llama, Gemma), third-party models, and Google's models on the same platform without vendor lock-in.
choice_c: Google Cloud provides AI services only to open-source projects.
choice_d: The open approach means there are no security restrictions on model usage.
explanation: Google Cloud's open approach means customers aren't locked into Google's proprietary models. Vertex AI's Model Garden supports open-source models (Llama, Mistral, Gemma), third-party models, and Google's own models — giving enterprises flexibility to choose the best model for their use case and avoid vendor lock-in.

stem: What is a Google Cloud TPU (Tensor Processing Unit) and why does it matter for AI?
choice_a: A TPU is Google's custom-designed AI accelerator chip optimised for matrix operations in neural network training and inference, offering significant performance and efficiency gains over general-purpose CPUs.
choice_b: A TPU is a type of cloud database optimised for storing tensor (multi-dimensional array) data.
choice_c: A TPU is a billing unit used to measure AI model training costs.
choice_d: A TPU is a translation processing unit used for language translation tasks.
explanation: Google's TPUs are custom ASICs (Application-Specific Integrated Circuits) designed specifically for the matrix multiplication operations that dominate neural network training and inference. TPUs offer higher throughput and better energy efficiency than GPUs for many AI workloads, giving Google a hardware advantage for training large models like Gemini.

stem: What is AutoML in the context of Google Cloud's democratisation of AI?
choice_a: A service that automatically runs marketing campaigns using AI
choice_b: A Vertex AI capability that automatically trains and tunes machine learning models from data with minimal ML expertise required
choice_c: A tool that automatically generates Python code for data scientists
choice_d: An automated model monitoring service that detects drift without configuration
explanation: AutoML enables organisations without deep ML expertise to build custom models by providing labelled data and letting the platform handle architecture selection, hyperparameter tuning, and training. It democratises AI by enabling business analysts and domain experts to create custom models, lowering the barrier to AI adoption.

stem: What are 'Gems' in the context of Gemini Advanced?
choice_a: Gems are Google's in-app currency used to purchase additional Gemini API credits.
choice_b: Gems are customisable AI personas or expert assistants within Gemini Advanced that users can configure with specific instructions, knowledge, and tone for recurring tasks.
choice_c: Gems are pre-built data connectors that link Gemini to enterprise databases.
choice_d: Gems are the individual responses generated by Gemini in a conversation.
explanation: Gems are personalised AI assistants within Gemini Advanced. Users configure a Gem with custom instructions (role, tone, knowledge context) to create a specialised assistant for recurring tasks — e.g., a 'Marketing Copy Gem', 'Code Review Gem', or 'Meeting Summariser Gem' — saving repeated prompting effort.

stem: What capability does Gemini Enterprise add that Gemini for Workspace does not include?
choice_a: The ability to use Gemini in Gmail and Docs
choice_b: Access to Gemini Pro model capabilities
choice_c: Enterprise-grade features including NotebookLM Enterprise API access, advanced multimodal search, and custom agent building capabilities at scale
choice_d: The ability to use Gemini in Google Meet
explanation: Gemini Enterprise extends beyond standard Workspace AI features to offer enterprise-grade capabilities: the NotebookLM Enterprise API (grounded research at scale), advanced multimodal search across enterprise content, and the ability to build and deploy custom Gemini-powered agents — targeted at large organisations with complex AI needs.

stem: What is Vertex AI Search and what problem does it solve?
choice_a: A tool for searching Vertex AI's model documentation
choice_b: An enterprise search solution that uses gen AI to deliver accurate, grounded search results across an organisation's unstructured and structured data sources
choice_c: A competitive intelligence tool for monitoring industry search trends
choice_d: A feature for searching the Model Garden for specific foundation models
explanation: Vertex AI Search enables organisations to build enterprise search engines powered by gen AI over their own data (documents, databases, websites). It returns AI-synthesised answers with citations rather than just document links, solving the problem of finding relevant information in large, siloed enterprise data repositories.

stem: What is 'Agent Assist' in Google's Customer Engagement Suite?
choice_a: A tool that fully replaces human contact centre agents with AI
choice_b: A real-time AI assistant that surfaces relevant information, suggested responses, and next-best actions to human agents during live customer conversations
choice_c: A service for automating email responses to customer enquiries
choice_d: A performance management tool for evaluating contact centre agent KPIs
explanation: Agent Assist uses AI to support human contact centre agents in real time — analysing the conversation, surfacing relevant knowledge base articles, suggesting responses, and recommending next-best actions. It improves agent productivity and response quality without removing humans from the interaction.

stem: What does 'Conversational Insights' provide in Google's Customer Engagement Suite?
choice_a: A chatbot that has conversations with customers to collect feedback
choice_b: AI-powered analytics that analyse customer conversations (calls, chats) to identify trends, sentiment, topics, and compliance issues at scale
choice_c: A tool for designing conversation flows in Dialogflow CX
choice_d: Real-time translation of customer conversations into different languages
explanation: Conversational Insights applies AI to analyse large volumes of contact centre interactions (recorded calls, chat transcripts) automatically — identifying common customer issues, sentiment trends, agent performance patterns, compliance risks, and improvement opportunities that would be impossible to detect through manual sampling.

stem: In the context of gen AI agents, what is an 'extension'?
choice_a: An additional subscription required to use advanced Vertex AI features
choice_b: A connector that allows an agent to interact with an external API or service in real time (e.g., calling a weather API, querying a database, or submitting a form)
choice_c: A method of extending a model's context window beyond its default limit
choice_d: A plugin that adds new programming languages to Gemini Code Assist
explanation: Extensions are tools that enable agents to take actions in the external world by calling APIs and services — fetching live data, writing to systems, executing transactions. They bridge the gap between an agent's reasoning capability and real-world systems, enabling agents to 'act' rather than just respond.

stem: What is the key distinction between Vertex AI Studio and Google AI Studio?
choice_a: Vertex AI Studio is free; Google AI Studio requires a paid subscription.
choice_b: Vertex AI Studio is Google Cloud's enterprise platform for building and deploying production AI applications with full GCP integration; Google AI Studio is a developer-focused, browser-based tool for rapid prototyping and experimenting with Gemini models.
choice_c: Google AI Studio supports more models than Vertex AI Studio.
choice_d: Vertex AI Studio is for image generation; Google AI Studio is for text generation.
explanation: Google AI Studio (ai.google.dev) is a free, lightweight browser tool for rapidly prototyping with Gemini — ideal for developers exploring model capabilities. Vertex AI Studio is part of Google Cloud's enterprise platform, offering production-grade features: security controls, IAM, VPC, model monitoring, MLOps pipelines, and billing integration — for building enterprise AI applications.

stem: Which Google Cloud API would a developer use to convert spoken customer calls into text for an AI agent to process?
choice_a: Cloud Vision API
choice_b: Natural Language API
choice_c: Document AI API
choice_d: Speech-to-Text API
explanation: The Speech-to-Text API converts audio input (spoken language) into text transcripts. For an AI agent processing customer phone calls, Speech-to-Text is the first step in the pipeline — converting audio to text before passing it to an LLM for understanding, sentiment analysis, or response generation.

stem: Which Google Cloud API is designed to extract structured information from documents like invoices, receipts, and forms?
choice_a: Natural Language API
choice_b: Cloud Vision API
choice_c: Document AI API
choice_d: Translation API
explanation: Document AI API uses AI to automatically extract structured data from semi-structured and unstructured documents — invoices, receipts, tax forms, contracts, medical records. It goes beyond OCR by understanding document structure and semantics, making it essential for automating document-heavy business processes.

stem: What is ReAct prompting and what makes it different from standard chain-of-thought?
choice_a: ReAct stands for 'Real-time Action' and is used for training models faster.
choice_b: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) interleaves reasoning steps with actions — the model reasons about what to do, takes an action (e.g., calling a tool or search), observes the result, then reasons again — enabling agents to complete multi-step tasks with external tools.
choice_c: ReAct is a variant of few-shot prompting that uses reactive examples.
choice_d: ReAct prompting eliminates the need for a system prompt by embedding instructions in the user turn.
explanation: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) is an advanced prompting technique where the model alternates between reasoning ('Thought') and taking actions ('Act') — such as searching the web, calling an API, or running code — then observing results ('Observation') before reasoning again. This enables LLMs to act as autonomous agents that complete tasks requiring multiple tool-use steps.

stem: What is 'prompt chaining' and when should it be used?
choice_a: Connecting multiple user devices in a chain to distribute AI compute
choice_b: Breaking a complex task into sequential prompts where the output of one prompt becomes the input of the next, enabling more reliable handling of multi-step tasks
choice_c: Combining multiple system prompts into a single merged instruction
choice_d: A technique for reusing prompt templates across different AI models
explanation: Prompt chaining decomposes a complex task into a pipeline of simpler subtasks. Each prompt handles one step, and its output feeds into the next prompt. This improves reliability (each step is simpler), enables better error handling, allows different models for different steps, and produces more structured final outputs than trying to do everything in one prompt.

stem: What is 'one-shot prompting' and how does it differ from zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
choice_a: One-shot prompting uses one word as the entire prompt.
choice_b: One-shot prompting provides exactly one example in the prompt to demonstrate the desired format or behaviour; zero-shot provides no examples; few-shot provides multiple examples (typically 3-5+).
choice_c: One-shot prompting trains the model on a single data point.
choice_d: One-shot prompting is faster than zero-shot because it uses fewer tokens.
explanation: The 'shot' count refers to the number of examples (demonstrations) included in the prompt. Zero-shot: no examples — relies entirely on model knowledge. One-shot: exactly one example — helps model understand desired format. Few-shot: multiple examples — provides richer guidance. More shots generally improve task performance but consume more context tokens.

stem: What is a 'knowledge cutoff' in foundation models and what is the recommended mitigation?
choice_a: The maximum number of tokens a model can generate in one response; mitigated by increasing max_tokens.
choice_b: The date after which training data was not included, meaning the model lacks knowledge of events after that date; mitigated by grounding with current data sources (RAG, Google Search).
choice_c: A safety filter that blocks certain types of knowledge from being shared with users.
choice_d: The point at which a model's accuracy drops when questions exceed its expertise area.
explanation: Foundation models have a training data cutoff — they have no knowledge of events, products, or research after that date. This is a fundamental limitation for time-sensitive use cases. The recommended mitigation is grounding: using RAG to retrieve current documents, or grounding with Google Search for real-time world information.

stem: What is the Vertex AI Feature Store used for?
choice_a: Storing and displaying model performance metrics in a dashboard
choice_b: A centralised repository for storing, sharing, and serving ML features consistently across training and serving, preventing training-serving skew
choice_c: A catalogue of pre-built ML model architectures
choice_d: A secure vault for storing API keys used by AI applications
explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store is a managed repository for ML features (transformed, engineered data inputs). By storing features centrally, it ensures the exact same feature transformations used during training are applied at serving time — preventing 'training-serving skew', a common cause of production model degradation. It also enables feature reuse across teams.

stem: What is the difference between grounding with 'first-party enterprise data' versus 'world data' in Google Cloud?
choice_a: First-party data is free; world data requires a paid Google subscription.
choice_b: First-party enterprise data grounds the model in the organisation's own private documents and databases; world data grounds the model in publicly available, real-time internet information via Google Search.
choice_c: World data is more accurate than first-party data for all enterprise use cases.
choice_d: First-party data can only be used with Gemini Nano, while world data works with all Gemini versions.
explanation: Grounding data sources serve different purposes. First-party (enterprise) data grounds responses in your organisation's proprietary knowledge — internal policies, product manuals, customer records. World data via Google Search grounds responses in current, publicly available internet information. Choosing the right grounding source depends on whether the query requires internal or external knowledge.

stem: Which Vertex AI grounding option provides real-time, publicly available information to ground model responses?
choice_a: RAG APIs with Vertex AI Vector Search
choice_b: Pre-built RAG with Vertex AI Search
choice_c: Grounding with Google Search
choice_d: Vertex AI Feature Store grounding
explanation: 'Grounding with Google Search' connects model responses to real-time public internet data via Google's search index.
