Unlike RAG with internal data stores, this option is ideal when responses need to reflect current events, recent product releases, or any information that changes frequently and isn't in enterprise data stores.

stem: What is the Security Command Center in Google Cloud's AI security context?
choice_a: A physical security facility where Google's AI hardware is protected
choice_b: Google Cloud's centralised security and risk management platform that provides visibility into security posture, threat detection, and vulnerability findings across GCP resources including AI workloads
choice_c: A team of Google security engineers who monitor customer AI deployments
choice_d: A dashboard for monitoring the content safety of AI model outputs
explanation: Security Command Center (SCC) is Google Cloud's unified security management platform. For AI workloads, SCC provides: continuous vulnerability scanning, threat detection across Vertex AI resources, misconfiguration identification, compliance reporting, and centralised visibility into the security posture of the entire AI lifecycle — from data storage to model deployment.

stem: Why is security important throughout the ML lifecycle rather than just at the deployment stage?
choice_a: Deploying a model is the most expensive stage, so security investment is maximised there.
choice_b: Each ML lifecycle stage introduces distinct risks: data poisoning during ingestion, model theft during training, adversarial inputs during serving, and privacy leakage in outputs — requiring security controls at every stage.
choice_c: Security is only relevant during the data ingestion phase because that's when external data enters the system.
choice_d: Modern AI frameworks handle all security automatically, so human oversight is only needed at deployment.
explanation: ML lifecycle security addresses stage-specific risks: data ingestion (data poisoning, supply chain attacks), data preparation (PII exposure), model training (IP theft, membership inference attacks), deployment (model extraction, adversarial attacks), and serving (prompt injection, output monitoring). A secure-by-design approach protects all stages.

stem: What is the difference between data anonymisation and data pseudonymisation in AI contexts?
choice_a: Anonymisation and pseudonymisation are interchangeable terms for the same process.
choice_b: Anonymisation irreversibly removes all identifying information so individuals cannot be re-identified; pseudonymisation replaces identifying information with tokens/codes that can be reversed with a separate key.
choice_c: Anonymisation is used for text data; pseudonymisation is used for image data.
choice_d: Pseudonymisation completely removes data, while anonymisation replaces it with fake data.
explanation: Anonymisation permanently removes all identifiers — the data can never be linked back to an individual (e.g., removing names, addresses, and combinations of attributes). Pseudonymisation replaces identifiers with tokens (e.g., replacing 'Jane Smith' with 'User_4829') — re-identification is possible with the mapping key. Anonymised data typically has fewer regulatory restrictions; pseudonymised data still requires protection.

stem: Which privacy technique should an organisation use when they need to share customer data with an AI model vendor for fine-tuning while retaining the ability to re-link results back to individual records?
choice_a: Anonymisation — permanently remove all identifiers before sharing
choice_b: Pseudonymisation — replace identifiers with tokens, retain the mapping key internally
choice_c: Encryption — encrypt all data fields before sharing
choice_d: Data deletion — delete all customer data before working with the vendor
explanation: Pseudonymisation is ideal when you need to share data for processing but retain the ability to re-link results. By replacing customer identifiers with tokens and keeping the mapping key secure internally, the vendor works with de-identified data (reducing risk), but you can still match model outputs back to specific customers for personalisation or reporting.

stem: When evaluating which type of gen AI solution to use, what is the primary factor determining whether to use a pre-built solution versus a custom-built one?
choice_a: The size of the organisation — large enterprises always need custom solutions.
choice_b: The specificity of the use case and availability of proprietary data — commodity tasks suit pre-built solutions; use cases requiring unique knowledge or behaviour suit custom solutions.
choice_c: The cloud provider — some providers only offer pre-built solutions.
choice_d: The number of users — pre-built solutions can only scale to 100 users.
explanation: Pre-built gen AI solutions (e.g., Gemini for Workspace, Vertex AI Search) are ideal for general-purpose tasks common across organisations. Custom-built solutions (fine-tuned models, bespoke RAG pipelines) are warranted when the use case requires proprietary knowledge, unique behaviour, or performance levels not achievable with off-the-shelf options — justified by higher cost and complexity.

stem: Which of the following is a recommended technique for measuring the business impact of a gen AI initiative?
choice_a: Counting the number of AI models deployed in production
choice_b: Measuring pre- and post-implementation KPIs directly tied to business outcomes (e.g., time-to-resolution, cost per transaction, customer satisfaction scores, revenue attributed to AI features)
choice_c: Tracking how many employees have completed AI training courses
choice_d: Monitoring the GPU utilisation rate of the AI infrastructure
explanation: Measuring gen AI impact requires establishing baseline business metrics before implementation and comparing them after. Relevant KPIs depend on the use case: contact centre AI → handle time and CSAT; code generation → developer velocity and bug rate; document processing → processing time and error rate. Technology metrics (model accuracy, latency) are intermediate, not final measures of business success.
